headline: Carlos gave mostly non-committal, short answers about the bar—what we have is a low-signal read on clarity and growth, not a shared standard yet.
summary_bullets: Carlos can’t reliably map wins to the team bar: “I can name wins, but I am not always sure they match the team bar.”
Under time pressure, his process shifts to polish over message: “I sometimes focus on polish and lose sight of the core message.”
When feedback is broad, he stalls on prioritization: “I am not sure what to change first,” and asks for “concept rationale” growth plus “one skill at a time.”
understanding_paragraph: We understood that Carlos defines progress as having concrete, criteria-based feedback that tells him what to change first. The session showed his main failure mode isn’t effort—it’s prioritization and decision rules under time pressure. He also didn’t commit to a specific next change before the next meeting, leaving the development plan unanchored.

axes.meaning: Weak signal: stress shows up as time-pressure inconsistency, worth watching but not enough to act on alone.
Weak signal: he’s trying to improve, but uncertainty about the bar reduces ownership and follow-through.
Defining signal: the team bar and feedback translation are unclear, so expectations feel subjective and unusable.
Real pattern: he’s blocked on concept rationale because feedback isn’t concrete enough to practice consistently.

brutal_truth_employee: Carlos is not getting a usable standard: “I can name wins, but I am not always sure they match the team bar.” He also admits the process breaks under pressure: “I sometimes focus on polish and lose sight of the core message.”
brutal_truth_manager: Carlos’s clarity signal is blunt across multiple turns (“team bar” mismatch, broad reviews, not knowing what to change first). The moment to deepen was after “When reviews are broad, I am not sure what to change first”—that’s where you should force a concrete priority rule and an example, not stay at “concept rationale” at a high level.

next_actions.action: Draft a one-page “team bar for junior brand design” with 3 criteria for concept rationale and 3 for execution, each with a do/don’t.
Schedule a 30-minute calibration session with Carlos to review one recent project and score it against the new criteria, deciding what changes first.
Re-ask the next-step question with a forced choice: “Pick one criterion to practice for 2 weeks—what will you change on your next deliverable?”

watch_for: Before next 1:1: if Carlos still can’t name what to change first after seeing concrete criteria, treat clarity as unresolved.
Within two weeks: if time-pressure work still drifts to “polish” over “core message,” the development focus needs a new decision rule.
